sn48.10:0.3: Sutta An Analysis of the Faculties
sn48.10:1.3: The faculty of conviction, the faculty of persistence, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, the faculty of discernment.
sn48.10:1.4: “Now what is the faculty of conviction?
sn48.10:1.5: There is the case where a monk, a disciple of the noble ones, has conviction, is convinced of the Tathāgata’s awakening:
sn48.10:1.6: ‘Indeed, the Blessed One is worthy & rightly self-awakened, consummate in clear-knowing & conduct, well-gone, an expert with regard to the cosmos, unexcelled trainer of people fit to be tamed, teacher of devas & human beings, awakened, blessed.’
sn48.10:1.7: This is called the faculty of conviction.
sn48.10:2.1: “And what is the faculty of persistence?
sn48.10:2.2: There is the case where a monk, a disciple of the noble ones, keeps his persistence aroused for abandoning unskillful mental qualities and taking on skillful mental qualities.
sn48.10:2.3: He is steadfast, solid in his effort, not shirking his duties with regard to skillful mental qualities. He generates desire, endeavors, arouses persistence, upholds & exerts his intent for the sake of the non-arising of evil, unskillful qualities that have not yet arisen…
sn48.10:2.4: for the sake of the abandoning of evil, unskillful qualities that have arisen…
sn48.10:2.5: for the sake of the arising of skillful qualities that have not yet arisen…
sn48.10:2.6: (and) for the maintenance, non-confusion, increase, plenitude, development, & culmination of skillful qualities that have arisen.
sn48.10:2.7: This is called the faculty of persistence.
sn48.10:3.1: “And what is the faculty of mindfulness?
sn48.10:3.2: There is the case where a monk, a disciple of the noble ones, is mindful, is endowed with excellent proficiency in mindfulness, remembering & able to call to mind even things that were done & said long ago.
sn48.10:3.3: He remains focused on the body in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn48.10:3.4: He remains focused on feelings in & of themselves…
sn48.10:3.6: mental qualities in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn48.10:3.7: This is called the faculty of mindfulness.
sn48.10:4.1: “And what is the faculty of concentration?
sn48.10:4.2: There is the case where a monk, a disciple of the noble ones, making it his object to let go, attains concentration, attains singleness of mind.
sn48.10:4.3: Quite secluded from sensuality, secluded from unskillful qualities—he enters & remains in the first jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of seclusion, accompanied by directed thought & evaluation.
sn48.10:4.4: With the stilling of directed thoughts & evaluations, he enters & remains in the second jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of concentration, unification of awareness free from directed thought & evaluation—internal assurance.
sn48.10:4.5: With the fading of rapture, he remains equanimous, mindful, & alert, and senses pleasure with the body. He enters & remains in the third jhāna, of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous & mindful, he has a pleasant abiding.’
sn48.10:4.6: With the abandoning of pleasure & pain—as with the earlier disappearance of elation & distress—he enters & remains in the fourth jhāna: purity of equanimity & mindfulness, neither pleasure nor pain.
sn48.10:4.7: This is called the faculty of concentration.
sn48.10:5.1: “And what is the faculty of discernment?
sn48.10:5.2: There is the case where a monk, a disciple of the noble ones, is discerning, endowed with discernment of arising & passing away—noble, penetrating, leading to the right ending of stress.
sn48.10:5.3: He discerns, as it has come to be: ‘This is stress…This is the origination of stress…This is the cessation of stress…This is the path of practice leading to the cessation of stress.’
sn48.10:5.4: This is called the faculty of discernment.
